title: checkpoint container only save tty 0
body:
### Issue Description

I opened a container and started two terminal devices, tty 0 and tty 1. The program running on tty 1 was a counting application. Then, I used podman checkpoint on this container. After executing podman restore, the memory information was not restored, and the counting program had been killed.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. podman run -it [image] bash
2. podman attach [containerId]
3. podman exec -it [containerId] bash
4. run a counting application
5. podman container checkpoint <container_id>
6. podman container restore <container_id>

### Describe the results you received

The memory information was not restored, and the counting program had been killed.

### Describe the results you expected

I opened a container and started two terminal devices, tty 0 and tty 1. The program running on tty 1 was a counting application. Then, I used `podman checkpoint` on this container. However, when I executed `podman restore`, the memory information was not restored, and the counting program had been killed. I would like to find a way to restore the memory information and resume the execution of the counting program on tty 1.

### podman info output

```yaml
no info
```


### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

comments.body: I don't think this works with criu. @adrianreber @rst0git PTAL

> I don't think this works with criu. @adrianreber @rst0git PTAL

Yes, you are right.

Containers with open TTYs will usually not work with checkpoint/restore. In theory that could be solved, but although restoring TTYs is possible it is something that should be avoided. Nice for demonstrations. I would not support this for containers.

The main problem, however, is that checkpointing does not work with `exec`. The process created by `exec` is not a child process of the containers PID 1. Checkpointing works by telling CRIU to checkpoint the container's PID 1 and all child processes. `exec` creates processes that are not PID 1 child processes and are not visible to CRIU. So that just does not work right now.

> I would like to find a way to restore the memory information and resume the execution of the counting program on tty 1.

Do not use open TTYs with checkpoint/restore. Do not use `exec` with checkpoint/restore.

> The main problem, however, is that checkpointing does not work with exec. The process created by exec is not a child process of the containers PID 1.

It is worth pointing out that this is a limitation of the container runtime ([runc](https://github.com/opencontainers/runc) / [crun](https://github.com/containers/crun)), not Podman.
Closing because it sounds like we cannot support this
